title: Floquet mobility edges and transport in a periodically driven generalized Aubry-André model
authors: Jayashis Das, Vatsana Tiwari, Manish Kumar, Auditya Sharma
abstract: We investigate the effect of a periodic electric field drive on the generalized Aubry-André model, also known as the Ganeshan-Pixley-Das Sarma (GPD) model, which is well known as a host of mobility edges. Our study of the Floquet spectrum of the driven GPD model uncovers the emergence of two distinct Floquet mobility edges, a delocalized--localized (DL) edge in the bounded regime, and a multifractal--localized (ML) edge in the unbounded regime. Using analytical results derived from Avila's global theory applied to the high frequency effective Hamiltonian, together with numerical diagnostics such as the fractal dimension and inverse participation ratio, we demonstrate that these mobility edges can be effectively controlled by the amplitude and frequency of the electric field drive. We also identify drive-induced localization at specific values of the driving parameters, corresponding to dynamical localization points in the absence of quasiperiodic potential. Furthermore, the dynamical study of the periodically driven GPD model demonstrates superdiffusive to almost ballistic transport in the bounded regime corresponding to the DL edges, whereas subdiffusive transport is observed in the unbounded regime associated with the ML edges. We also analyze deviations from the high-frequency effective description by explicitly examining the low-frequency driving regime, where significant and counterintuitive deviations in both spectral properties and transport behavior are observed. Our study highlights the interplay of a quasiperiodic potential and a periodically varying electric field drive as a powerful mechanism to engineer mobility edges and control transport in systems with rich spectral features.
summary: This paper explores how a periodic electric field can be used to tune the localization properties of a quasiperiodic system. The authors identify two types of Floquet mobility edges depending on the model's deformation parameter. They show that the driving amplitude can induce full localization at specific points and that the driving frequency can switch transport between superdiffusive and subdiffusive regimes. This work highlights the potential for using periodic driving to precisely engineer quantum transport in disordered systems.
main_problem: Investigating how a periodic electric field drive modifies the mobility edge structure and transport properties in the generalized Aubry-Andre (GPD) model.
main_result: The study uncovers two distinct Floquet mobility edges (delocalized-localized and multifractal-localized) and demonstrates that driving parameters can control transport regimes ranging from superdiffusive to subdiffusive.
method: The authors use Floquet-Magnus expansion and Avila's global theory for analytical predictions, alongside numerical diagnostics like fractal dimension, IPR, and real-time wave packet dynamics.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional periodically driven Ganeshan-Pixley-Das Sarma (GPD) model describing non-interacting spinless fermions on a quasiperiodic lattice subject to a time-periodic electric field.
key_observables: Fractal dimension (D2), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), Lyapunov exponent, root-mean-squared deviation (RMSD), and return probability.
important_parameters: Driving amplitude (K), driving frequency (omega), potential strength (lambda), and the deformation parameter (beta).
main_assumptions: High-frequency limit approximations are used for the effective Hamiltonian; higher-order terms in the Floquet-Magnus expansion are neglected in certain analytical derivations.
figures_summary: Figures show the fractal dimension as a function of eigenenergy and driving strength, the scaling of IPR to extract fractal dimensions, and the time evolution of return probability and wave packet spreading.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the driven GPD model, applies Floquet-Magnus expansion and Avila's theory to identify mobility edges, analyzes spectral properties (fractal dimension/IPR), investigates transport dynamics (RMSD/diffusion), and discusses frequency-dependent deviations.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful mechanism for engineering mobility edges and controlling transport via external driving, which is highly relevant for controlling localization in driven many-body systems and synthetic quantum lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fractal dimension D2 of all the eigenstates of the undriven GPD model as a function of eigenenergies E and quasiperiodic potential strength λ. (a) For β = −0.8, where extended and localized eigenstates coexist and are separated by a DL edge. (b) For β = 2.0, where the extended phase is replaced by a multifractal phase, leading to an ML edge. Black dashed lines denote the exact analytical mobility edges given in Eq. (4) and (5). Results are shown for a system size L = 987 with periodic boundary conditions, averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.

FIG. 2. Fractal dimension D2 of all the Floquet eigenstates of the driven GPD model at ω = 10, shown as a function of quasienergy ϵ and K. (a) For β = −0.8, where periodic driving induces a DL edge in the Floquet spectrum. (b) For β = 2.0, where the system exhibits an ML edge, with multifractal states (0 < D2 < 1) in the central region and localized states near the spectral edges. Black dashed lines denote the ana- lytically obtained mobility edges in Eq. (19). At the zeros of J0(K), occurring at K ≈2.4048, 5.5201, 8.6537, . . . (marked by yellow crosses), the effective hopping is suppressed, result- ing in a fully localized spectrum. The arrows mark repre- sentative points whose spectral...

FIG. 3. Sorted inverse participation ratio I2 of Floquet eigenstates as a function of the normalized eigenstate index n/L for different system sizes at β = −0.8 (with DL edges), shown for different values of K, where K2 denotes the second zero of J0(K). (a)–(c) correspond to ω = 10, while (d)–(f) correspond to ω = 2. Insets show the average ⟨I2⟩over Floquet eigenstates within the grey shaded window as a function of system size. The fractal exponent D2 is extracted from linear fits of log⟨I2⟩versus log L, as defined in Eq. (23). All results are for λ = 0.05, averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.

FIG. 4. Sorted inverse participation ratio I2 of Floquet eigenstates as a function of the normalized eigenstate index n/L for different system sizes at β = 2.0 (exhibiting ML edges), shown for different values of K, where K2 denotes the second zero of J0(K). (a)-(c) correspond to ω = 10, while (d)-(f) correspond to ω = 2. Insets show the average ⟨I2⟩over Floquet eigenstates within the grey shaded window as a function of system size. The fractal exponent D2 is extracted from linear fits of log⟨I2⟩ versus log L, as defined in Eq. (23). All the results are for λ = 0.05, and averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.

FIG. 5. Standard deviation σ of Floquet eigenstates as a function of the normalized eigenstate index n/L for β = −0.8 (associated with DL edges), shown for different values of K. (a)-(c) correspond to ω = 10, while (d)-(f) correspond to ω = 2. Black dashed lines denote the analytical mobility edge positions obtained from Eq. (19). At the Bessel zero K = K2 [panels (b) and (e)], all states are localized. Away from this point [panels (a), (c), (d), and (f)], extended states dominate the spectrum, with localized states appearing near the lower- edge of the spectrum. Results are shown for a system size L = 987, λ = 0.05, and averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.

FIG. 6. Standard deviation σ of Floquet eigenstates as a function of the normalized eigenstate index n/L for β = 2.0 (associated with ML edges), shown for different values of K. (a)-(c) correspond to ω = 10, while (d)-(f) correspond to ω = 2. Black dashed lines denote the analytical mobility edge positions obtained from Eq. (19). At the Bessel zero K = K2 [panels (b) and (e)], all states are localized. Away from this point [panels (a), (c), (d), and (f)], extended states dominate the spectrum, with localized states appearing near the lower-energy edge. Results are shown for a system size L = 987, λ = 0.05, and averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.

FIG. 7. Dynamics of the root-mean-squared displacement X(t) of a wave packet initially localized at the center of the chain. Rows correspond to different values of the driving fre- quency ω, while columns correspond to different values of β. Different values of K are represented by distinct markers and colors, with K2 ≈5.52 denoting the second zero of the Bessel function J0(K). Black dashed lines indicate power-law fits of X(t) ∼tγ in the long-time regime, after initial transients and before saturation. The extracted growth exponents γ, cor- responding to each value of K, are indicated by the respec- tive markers. Results are shown for a system size L = 987, λ = 0.05, and averaged over 100...

FIG. 8. Dynamics of the half-chain entanglement entropy S(t) for the periodically driven GPD model. Rows corre- spond to different values of the driving frequency ω, while columns correspond to different values of β. Different val- ues of K are represented by distinct markers and colors, with K2 ≈5.52 denoting the second zero of the Bessel function J0(K). Black dashed lines denote power-law fits of S(t) ∼tν

FIG. 9. Fractal dimension D2 of all the Floquet eigenstates of the driven GPD model for ω = 10 shown as a function of quasienergy ϵ and K. (a),(c) For periodic boundary conditions (PBC) with β = −0.8 and β = 2.0, respectively. (b),(d) For open boundary conditions (OBC) with β = −0.8 and β = 2.0, respectively. Black dashed lines denote the analytically obtained mobility edges in Eq. (19). At the zeros of J0(K) (marked by yellow crosses), drive-induced localization leads to a fully localized spectrum. Results are shown for a system size L = 987, λ = 0.05, and averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.

FIG. 11. Average fractal dimension ⟨D2⟩of all eigenstates of the effective Hamiltonian in Eq. (13), plotted as a function of quasiperiodic potential strength λ and K for (a) β = −0.8, and (b) β = 2.0. Results are shown for a system size L = 987, averaged over 10 realizations of the phase ϕ.
